questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between point-to-point (queue) and publish/subscribe (topic) messaging?
options: Point-to-point is faster; pub/sub is more reliable.
In point-to-point, each message is delivered to exactly one consumer in a group; in pub/sub, every subscriber receives every message.
Point-to-point requires a broker; pub/sub does not.
Pub/sub guarantees ordering; point-to-point does not.
questions.explanation: Point-to-point (queue) distributes work: each message goes to one consumer, enabling parallel processing. Publish/subscribe (topic) broadcasts events: every subscriber independently receives every message. The choice depends on whether you want work distribution (point-to-point) or event notification (pub/sub).

questions.question: Why does Kafka guarantee FIFO ordering only per partition rather than globally across a topic?
options: Kafka brokers don't have enough memory to maintain global order.
Global ordering requires coordinating all writes through a single partition, which limits throughput to what one broker can handle.
Consumers can't handle globally ordered messages.
Kafka uses eventual consistency, so global ordering isn't possible.
questions.explanation: A topic with a single partition gives total ordering but bottleneck throughput on one broker. Adding partitions increases throughput by distributing writes across brokers, but breaks global ordering. Messages within a partition are FIFO, and messages with the same key always go to the same partition, so per-key ordering is preserved.

questions.question: In a Kafka consumer group with 3 consumers reading a 6-partition topic, what happens when one consumer crashes?
options: The crashed consumer's messages are lost.
The remaining two consumers each take over half of the crashed consumer's partitions through rebalancing, and consumption continues after a brief pause.
A new consumer is automatically created to replace the crashed one.
The topic is reduced to 4 partitions to match the remaining consumers.
questions.explanation: Kafka consumer groups redistribute partitions among surviving consumers when membership changes (this is rebalancing). The two remaining consumers take over the crashed consumer's 2 partitions, giving them 3 partitions each instead of 2. During rebalancing, consumption pauses briefly. No messages are lost — they remain in the partitions with their committed offsets.

questions.question: You need at-least-once delivery semantics in Kafka. What must you ensure?
options: Set producer acks=0 and consumer auto-commit enabled.
Set producer acks=all and make consumers idempotent, committing offsets only after processing.
Use a single partition and exactly-once transactions.
Disable consumer groups so each consumer reads all partitions.
questions.explanation: At-least-once delivery requires: (1) The producer waits for acknowledgment from all in-sync replicas (acks=all) so messages aren't lost. (2) The consumer processes messages before committing offsets. If the consumer crashes after processing but before committing, it re-reads those messages — hence duplicates. Making consumers idempotent (processing the same message twice has the same effect as once) handles these duplicates.

questions.question: When would you choose NATS core over Kafka?
options: When you need to replay messages from a historical log.
When you need exactly-once delivery for financial transactions.
When you need sub-millisecond latency for real-time fan-out and can tolerate at-most-once delivery (messages may be lost if no subscriber is connected).
When you need durable storage for regulatory compliance.
questions.explanation: NATS core is fire-and-forget: if no subscriber is connected, messages are dropped. This makes it ideal for real-time fan-out (market data, live telemetry) where latency matters more than durability. Kafka's strength is durable, replayable logs — the opposite trade-off. If you need both speed and durability with NATS, you'd use JetStream, not core.

questions.question: What makes exactly-once semantics expensive in Kafka?
options: It requires all consumers to read from a single partition to avoid duplicates.

It requires idempotent producers (sequence numbers per partition) and transactional consumer-read-process-write cycles, where the consumer only reads committed transaction markers — adding coordination overhead and reducing throughput.
It requires ZooKeeper to maintain a global deduplication index across all brokers.
Exactly-once is impossible in distributed systems, so you should always use at-most-once instead.
questions.explanation: Exactly-once in Kafka requires: (1) Idempotent producers that assign sequence numbers per producer-partition pair to detect and discard duplicates. (2) Transactional producers that group multiple sends and a commit marker into an atomic transaction. (3) Transactional consumers that only read messages from committed transactions. This coordination across brokers and the commit protocol adds significant latency and reduces throughput — which is why most systems use at-least-once with idempotent consumers instead.
